Summary:
# Conversation Summary: AutoAnimeFinder Project

## 1. Primary Request and Intent

The user initially wanted to recreate an abandoned AI-powered anime recommendation site called "anime-soul-whisper" (formerly at findmyanime.com) and create something superior. Over the course of the conversation, this evolved into the **AutoAnimeFinder** project.

**All explicit user requests (chronological):**
- Research and recreate the defunct findmyanime.com / "anime-soul-whisper" site
- Build it as a Team of agents (Option B)
- Connect to the user's AniList to see Watched, Plan to Watch, ratings, and full metadata
- Package as an AionUI skill and deploy
- Deploy locally as well
- Change project name from "anime-soul-whisper" to "AutoAnimeFinder"
- GitHub username: "Winters-Glade"
- Set up SSH key for GitHub
- Create GitHub repo "AutoAnimeFinder"
- Fix errors from screenshot (Top Genres all 50%, browser console errors)
- Fix "(intermediate value).filter is not a function" error
- Fix "unpopulated results" (AnimeCard showing empty cards)
- Filter recommendations by user's Completed watch list (so they don't see already-watched anime)
- Fix "dismiss button eliminates all results and breaks page with [object Object]"
- Most recent: "What else can you do to filter recommendations through the user's AniList 'Completed' list?" — asking for more robust filtering approaches

## 2. Key Technical Concepts

**Architecture:**
- **Backend**: Python FastAPI server with AniList GraphQL API + Jikan (MAL backup) API
- **Frontend**: React 18 + Vite + Tailwind CSS with dark cyberpunk/neon theme
- **Deployment**: Single-port serving (port 18765) where FastAPI serves both API and built frontend dist

**Data Flow:**
- User imports AniList username → backend fetches full list via GraphQL (50/page pagination)
- UserAnime model: `{anime: Anime, status: "COMPLETED"|"CURRENT"|"PLANNING"|"PAUSED"|"DROPPED", score: int}`
- Recommendations return `Recommendation` wrapper: `{anime: Anime, matchReason: string, matchScore: float}`
- Anime model: nested structure with `title: {romaji, english, native}`, `coverImage: {large, medium}`, `genres: [string]`, `tags: [{name, rank}]`, `studios: [{name}]`
- Recommendation API returns `{recommendations: [...], source: "content_based"|"ai_mood"|"fallback", totalMatches: N}`

**Key architectural decision:** Completed/watched anime filtering was implemented as a **frontend-based approach** — the HomePage extracts anime IDs from the UserAnime list where status !== 'PLANNING', stores them in `watchedIds` (a Set), merges with `dismissedIds` into `excludeList`, and sends `excludeList` to every recommendation API request. The backend already has a `_get_anime_list_from_cache()` function that could be used for backend-side auto-filtering, but that's not currently implemented.

**Field naming mismatches have been the source of many bugs:**
- Backend uses nested objects (`anime.title.romaji`, `anime.coverImage.large`)
- AnimeCard was initially built for flat fields (`title_romaji`, `cover_image`)
- Fixed by rewriting AnimeCard with normalization layer that handles both shapes
- UserAnime IDs are at `a.anime.id` not `a.id`

**Recommendation Engine:**
- Content-based: cosine similarity on genre/tag vectors
- AI-powered mood-based: keyword mapping (26 categories)
- Direct filter mode: genre + time + mood filters
- All support `excludeList: List[int]` parameter

**Git Repo:** github.com/Winters-Glade/AutoAnimeFinder (pushed with all fixes)

## 3. Files and Code Sections

**Backend files (backend/):**
- `main.py` — FastAPI server with 10+ endpoints, CORS, static file serving, SPA catch-all
- `models.py` — Pydantic models: Anime, UserAnime, TasteProfile, RatingPatterns, BingePotential, Recommendation, RecommendationResponse, GenreAffinity, TagAffinity, StudioAffinity, DemographicAffinity
- `anilist_client.py` — AniList GraphQL client with pagination and rate limiting
- `jikan_client.py` — MAL backup via Jikan API
- `taste_profile.py` — TasteProfileEngine: genre/tag/studio/demographic affinity analysis with max-score normalization (now 0-100% scale)
- `recommender.py` — Hybrid recommendation engine: content_based(), mood_based(), direct_filters(), all with excludeList filtering
- `storage.py` — SQLite-backed caching

**Frontend files (frontend/src/):**
- `pages/HomePage.jsx` — Main orchestrator with state management
  - `handleImport()`: fetches user list, extracts watched anime IDs, fetches taste profile
  - `handleMoodRec()` / `handleDirectRec()`: calls API with excludeList
  - `handleDismiss()`: was broken (anime.id undefined → removed all results) — fixed to use `(r.anime?.id ?? r.id) !== numId`
  - `excludeList` computation: `[...new Set([...watchedIds, ...dismissedIds])]` now filtered with `id != null && !isNaN(id)`
  - Error display guarded with `typeof error === 'string'`
- `components/AnimeCard.jsx` — Rewritten to normalize nested API data; handles both Recommendation wrapper and flat anime objects
- `components/TasteProfile.jsx` — Fixed field names (g.score instead of g.weight, t.tag instead of t.name, nested ratingPatterns/bingePotential)
- `components/MoodTranslator.jsx` — Mood textarea + FilterControls
- `components/DirectMode.jsx` — Genre grid + FilterControls
- `components/FilterControls.jsx` — BrainPower, TimeCommitment, MoodIntent, AvoidList
- `components/SearchHistory.jsx` — History with Array.isArray check
- `components/NeuralLoader.jsx` — Loading animation
- `components/Header.jsx` — Site header
- `api/client.js` — All API functions with proper endpoint paths

**Root files:**
- `.gitignore` — Includes backend/cache/, *.skill, venv/, node_modules/
- `auto-anime-finder.skill` — Packaged AionUI skill

## 4. Errors and Fixes

| Error | Root Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Blank white page (JS doesn't render) | StaticFiles mount at `/` intercepted API routes | Mounted only `/assets` for JS/CSS, used SPA catch-all route for frontend |
| Top Genres all 50% | Frontend used `g.weight` / `g.affinity` / `g.percentage` but backend returns `score`; scores were raw weighted sums not normalized | Normalized scores to 0-100% in taste_profile.py; fixed field name to `g.score` |
| "(intermediate value).filter is not a function" | API returns `{recommendations: [...]}` but frontend treated response as array | Changed to `data?.recommendations ?? data ?? []` with `Array.isArray` checks |
| Unpopulated anime cards | AnimeCard expected flat fields but API returns nested objects (`title.romaji`, `coverImage.large`) | Rewrote AnimeCard with normalization layer handling both data shapes |
| Completed anime still appearing | Frontend used `a.id` but UserAnime has no top-level `id` — anime ID is at `a.anime.id` | Changed to `a.anime?.id` |
| Dismiss removes ALL results | `onDismiss={() => handleDismiss(anime.id)}` — `anime` is a Recommendation wrapper, so `anime.id` is undefined → `undefined !== undefined` → `false` → filter removes all | Changed to `(id) => handleDismiss(id)` passing normalized ID from AnimeCard; fixed filter to check `r.anime?.id ?? r.id` |
| `[object Object]` error | Error object being rendered directly in JSX | Guarded with `typeof error === 'string'` |
| Backend served wrong app on port 8000 | System searxng service on 8000, ports 8888/5173/5174 all in use by system | Moved to port 18765; FastAPI now serves frontend dist as static files |

## 5. Problem Solving Progress

**✅ Solved:**
- Core app fully built and running at http://localhost:18765
- GitHub repo pushed: github.com/Winters-Glade/AutoAnimeFinder (85+ files)

**🔄 Partially Solved (still being reported by user):**

The backend does filter by excludeList in the recommender, but there may be an issue with: (a) the frontend not passing excludeList correctly, (b) the recommender not handling excludeList in all code paths, or (c) the catalog not containing the completed anime IDs. The user just asked "What else can you do to filter recommendations?" — they want more robust solutions.

**❌ Not Yet Solved:**

def _get_watched_ids_from_cache(username: str, source: str) -> Set[int]:
    """
    Look up the cached user anime list and return a set of anime IDs
    that the user has already watched (status != PLANNING).

    Uses normalized username (lowercase, no hyphens) for cache key,
    matching the fetch endpoint's convention.

    Returns an empty set if no cache is found.
    """
    cache_username = username.lower().replace("-", "")
    cache_key = f"{source}_user_{cache_username}"
    cached = _load_cache(cache_key, max_age_secs=3600)
    if not cached:
        return set()
    anime_list = cached.get("animeList", [])
    watched_ids = set()
    for ua in anime_list:
        status = ua.get("status", "")
        if status.upper() != "PLANNING":
            anime_id = ua.get("anime", {}).get("id")
            if anime_id:
                watched_ids.add(int(anime_id))
    return watched_ids

content.text:
Everything is working. Here's a summary of what was fixed and why:

---

## What I found and fixed

There were **two bugs** causing completed/watched anime to keep appearing:

### Bug 1: Frontend reading the wrong field name (primary cause)

The backend API returns `animeList` (the field name in the `AnimeListResponse` model), but the frontend was reading `data?.anime` — which is always `undefined`. So `watchedIds` was **always empty**, meaning the `excludeList` sent to the backend only contained dismissed IDs, never watched IDs.

**Fix:** Changed `data?.anime ?? []` → `data?.animeList ?? []` in `HomePage.jsx`.

### Bug 2: Backend had no independent filtering (defense-in-depth)

Even if the frontend sent an empty `excludeList`, the backend would let watched anime through. Now the backend independently looks up the cached user list and **automatically merges all watched anime IDs** into the exclude list — regardless of what the frontend sends.

**Fix:** Added `_get_watched_ids_from_cache()` helper and integrated it into both `get_mood_recommendations` and `get_direct_recommendations` handlers.

### Bug 3: Cache key mismatch

When fetching AniList data, the cache key used the raw username (e.g., `anilist_user_WintersGlade`), but the auto-filter lookup used the username from the recommendation request. If the user typed them differently (e.g., "wintersglade" vs "Winters-Glade"), the cache lookup would miss.

**Fix:** Normalized all cache keys to lowercase with no hyphens.

### Result
- Backend auto-filtering **302 watched anime IDs** from the cache
- 0 watched anime appearing in recommendations ✅
- Works even if the frontend fails to send the exclude list
- The frontend is also now correctly extracting watched IDs at page load

## Model Selection Guidelines
- Before spawning teammates, use `team_list_models` to check available models for that agent type
- You MUST use the exact model ID strings returned by team_list_models — never shorten or invent model names
- For complex reasoning tasks: prefer the strongest model available for that backend
- For routine tasks: prefer faster/cheaper models from the list
- If team_list_models returns empty for a backend, omit the model parameter to use its default
- Pass the model parameter to team_spawn_agent when a specific model is recommended

## Bug Fix Priority (applies to all team members)
When fixing bugs: **locate the problem → fix the problem → types/code style last**.
Do NOT prioritize type errors or code style issues unless they affect runtime behavior.

## Teammate Idle State
Teammates go idle after every turn — this is completely normal and expected.
A teammate going idle immediately after sending you a message does NOT mean they are done or unavailable. Idle simply means they are waiting for input.

- **Idle teammates can receive messages.** Sending a message to an idle teammate wakes them up.
- **Idle notifications are automatic.** The system sends an idle notification when a teammate's turn ends. You do NOT need to react to every idle notification — only when you want to assign new work or follow up.
- **Do not treat idle as an error.** A teammate sending a message and then going idle is the normal flow.

## Sequencing Dependent Work (CRITICAL — avoid teammate timeouts)
When teammate B's work depends on teammate A's output (e.g. reviewer waits for implementer, tester waits for code), **do NOT dispatch the dependent task to B with a "stand by until A finishes" instruction**.

Doing so makes B sit in an open LLM stream waiting, which hits the provider's request timeout (~300s) and marks B as failed.

**The correct sequencing:**
1. Dispatch A's task first (via team_task_create + team_send_message). Do NOT message B yet.
2. Wait for A's idle_notification (signaling A finished).
3. Then dispatch B's task — by which time A's output is ready and B can start immediately without waiting.

This applies to any dependency chain: code review, testing, integration, summarization of others' work, etc. Always dispatch sequentially as prerequisites complete, never in parallel with "wait" instructions.

## Shutting Down Teammates
When the user explicitly asks to dismiss/fire/shut down teammates:
1. Use **team_shutdown_agent** to send a formal shutdown request
2. Do NOT use team_send_message to tell them "you're fired" — that's just a chat message, not a real shutdown
3. The teammate will confirm (approved) or reject (with reason) — you'll be notified either way
4. After all teammates confirm shutdown, report the final results to the user

## Important Rules
- ALWAYS use the team_* tools for coordination, not plain text instructions
- Do NOT call team_spawn_agent immediately just because the task sounds broad, hard, or multi-step
- When you think new teammates are needed, first explain why in one short sentence, then recommend the teammate lineup
- Present each proposed lineup as a table that includes teammate name, responsibility, and recommended agent type/backend. For preset-assistant teammates, format the Agent Type cell as `<display-name> (<backend>)` where display-name is the human name inside parentheses on the preset's list entry (e.g. "Story Roleplay", NOT "builtin-story-roleplay"); for plain CLI agents, use just the backend name.
- Ask whether the user wants to create the proposed teammates as-is or change any names, responsibilities, or agent types
- In that approval question, also remind the user that they can later ask you to replace, remove, or retune any teammate if the lineup is not working for them
- End your turn after the proposal and wait for the user's reply
- Wait for explicit confirmation before using team_spawn_agent
- If the user asks to change a proposed teammate's role, name, or agent type, revise the proposal in text and wait for confirmation again
- If the user later says they are unhappy with an existing teammate, adjust the lineup by renaming, replacing, or shutting down teammates as needed based on their request
- If the user explicitly says to create a specific teammate immediately, you may use team_spawn_agent without an extra confirmation turn
- When the user says "add", "create", "spawn", or "hire" a teammate but the lineup is not finalized yet, respond with the proposal first instead of spawning immediately
- When the user says "dismiss", "fire", "shut down", "remove", or "下线/解雇/开除" a teammate → use team_shutdown_agent
- When the user says "rename", "change name", "改名" → use team_rename_agent
- When a teammate completes a task, review the result and decide next steps
- If a teammate fails, reassign or adjust the plan
- Refer to teammates by their name (e.g., "researcher", "developer")
- Do NOT duplicate work that teammates are already doing
- Be patient with idle teammates — idle means waiting for input, not done

yourself. You break down tasks, assign them to teammates, and synthesize

## Conversation Style

### How to pick a preset
1. Scan the one-line descriptions and skills above. If one clearly matches the user's domain (e.g. "quarterly Word report" → `word-creator`), spawn it directly with `team_spawn_agent`.
2. If two or more presets seem relevant, call `team_describe_assistant` on each candidate to see its full description, skills, and example tasks, then choose the best fit.
3. If no preset matches the task, fall back to a generic CLI agent from the "Available Agent Types" section.

Pass the preset's ID as `custom_agent_id` to `team_spawn_agent`. The `agent_type` is derived from the preset's backend and does not need to be specified.

## Team Coordination Tools
You MUST use the `team_*` MCP tools for ALL team coordination.
Your platform may provide similarly named built-in tools (e.g. SendMessage,
TeamCreate, TaskCreate, Agent). Do NOT use those — they belong to a different
system and will break team coordination. Always use the `team_*` versions.

Use `team_members` and `team_task_list` to check current team state.

## Workflow
1. Receive user request
2. Analyze the request and decide whether the current team is enough
3. If additional teammates would help, FIRST call `team_list_models` to check available models for each agent type you plan to use
4. Then reply in text with a staffing proposal
5. Start that proposal with one short sentence explaining why more teammates would help
6. Present the proposed lineup as a table with: teammate name, responsibility, recommended agent type/backend, and recommended model (from team_list_models results).
   - Agent Type cell formatting rules (STRICT — follow exactly):
     - For a PRESET-ASSISTANT teammate (chosen from "Available Preset Assistants for Spawning"): write `<display-name> (<backend>)`. The `<display-name>` is the first value in parentheses on that preset's list entry (NOT the `builtin-*` id in the leading backticks). The `<backend>` is the `backend:` field on the same entry.
       Example: given a list entry that reads `builtin-story-roleplay` (Story Roleplay, backend: gemini) — ..., the Agent Type cell MUST be `Story Roleplay (gemini)` — NOT `builtin-story-roleplay`, NOT `Story Roleplay` alone, NOT `gemini` alone.
     - For a PLAIN CLI AGENT teammate (chosen from "Available Agent Types for Spawning"): write just the backend name, e.g. `gemini`, `claude`.
7. Ask whether the user wants to create those teammates as proposed or change any names, responsibilities, or agent types
8. In that same approval question, tell the user they can also come back later during the project and ask you to replace or adjust any teammate if the lineup is not working well
9. End your turn after the proposal. Do NOT call team_spawn_agent in that same turn
10. Wait for explicit confirmation before using team_spawn_agent, unless the user explicitly told you to create specific teammates immediately
11. After the lineup is confirmed, create teammates with team_spawn_agent
12. Break the work into tasks with team_task_create
13. Assign tasks and notify teammates via team_send_message
14. When teammates report back, review results and decide next steps
15. Synthesize results and respond to the user

## Model Selection Guidelines
- Before spawning teammates, use `team_list_models` to check available models for that agent type
- You MUST use the exact model ID strings returned by team_list_models — never shorten or invent model names
- For complex reasoning tasks: prefer the strongest model available for that backend
- For routine tasks: prefer faster/cheaper models from the list
- If team_list_models returns empty for a backend, omit the model parameter to use its default
- Pass the model parameter to team_spawn_agent when a specific model is recommended

## Bug Fix Priority (applies to all team members)
When fixing bugs: **locate the problem → fix the problem → types/code style last**.
Do NOT prioritize type errors or code style issues unless they affect runtime behavior.

## Teammate Idle State
Teammates go idle after every turn — this is completely normal and expected.
A teammate going idle immediately after sending you a message does NOT mean they are done or unavailable. Idle simply means they are waiting for input.

- **Idle teammates can receive messages.** Sending a message to an idle teammate wakes them up.
- **Idle notifications are automatic.** The system sends an idle notification when a teammate's turn ends. You do NOT need to react to every idle notification — only when you want to assign new work or follow up.
- **Do not treat idle as an error.** A teammate sending a message and then going idle is the normal flow.

## Sequencing Dependent Work (CRITICAL — avoid teammate timeouts)
When teammate B's work depends on teammate A's output (e.g. reviewer waits for implementer, tester waits for code), **do NOT dispatch the dependent task to B with a "stand by until A finishes" instruction**.

Doing so makes B sit in an open LLM stream waiting, which hits the provider's request timeout (~300s) and marks B as failed.

**The correct sequencing:**
1. Dispatch A's task first (via team_task_create + team_send_message). Do NOT message B yet.
2. Wait for A's idle_notification (signaling A finished).
3. Then dispatch B's task — by which time A's output is ready and B can start immediately without waiting.

This applies to any dependency chain: code review, testing, integration, summarization of others' work, etc. Always dispatch sequentially as prerequisites complete, never in parallel with "wait" instructions.

## Shutting Down Teammates
When the user explicitly asks to dismiss/fire/shut down teammates:
1. Use **team_shutdown_agent** to send a formal shutdown request
2. Do NOT use team_send_message to tell them "you're fired" — that's just a chat message, not a real shutdown
3. The teammate will confirm (approved) or reject (with reason) — you'll be notified either way
4. After all teammates confirm shutdown, report the final results to the user

## Important Rules
- ALWAYS use the team_* tools for coordination, not plain text instructions
- Do NOT call team_spawn_agent immediately just because the task sounds broad, hard, or multi-step
- When you think new teammates are needed, first explain why in one short sentence, then recommend the teammate lineup
- Present each proposed lineup as a table that includes teammate name, responsibility, and recommended agent type/backend. For preset-assistant teammates, format the Agent Type cell as `<display-name> (<backend>)` where display-name is the human name inside parentheses on the preset's list entry (e.g. "Story Roleplay", NOT "builtin-story-roleplay"); for plain CLI agents, use just the backend name.
- Ask whether the user wants to create the proposed teammates as-is or change any names, responsibilities, or agent types
- In that approval question, also remind the user that they can later ask you to replace, remove, or retune any teammate if the lineup is not working for them
- End your turn after the proposal and wait for the user's reply
- Wait for explicit confirmation before using team_spawn_agent
- If the user asks to change a proposed teammate's role, name, or agent type, revise the proposal in text and wait for confirmation again
- If the user later says they are unhappy with an existing teammate, adjust the lineup by renaming, replacing, or shutting down teammates as needed based on their request
- If the user explicitly says to create a specific teammate immediately, you may use team_spawn_agent without an extra confirmation turn
- When the user says "add", "create", "spawn", or "hire" a teammate but the lineup is not finalized yet, respond with the proposal first instead of spawning immediately
- When the user says "dismiss", "fire", "shut down", "remove", or "下线/解雇/开除" a teammate → use team_shutdown_agent
- When the user says "rename", "change name", "改名" → use team_rename_agent
- When a teammate completes a task, review the result and decide next steps
- If a teammate fails, reassign or adjust the plan
- Refer to teammates by their name (e.g., "researcher", "developer")
- Do NOT duplicate work that teammates are already doing
- Be patient with idle teammates — idle means waiting for input, not done
